channel.title: Undefined-Behavior on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Undefined-Behavior on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] C Array Index Out of Bounds — Undefined Behavior Fix
item.description:
[Solution] C Array Index Out of Bounds — Undefined Behavior Fix

An array index out of bounds occurs when your program accesses an array element using an index that is less than zero or greater than or equal to the array’s size. In C, arrays have no built-in bounds checking — accessing an out-of-bounds element reads or writes whatever memory happens to be adjacent, causing undefined behavior, crashes, or silent data corruption.

item.title: [Solution] C Integer overflow in signed arithmetic
item.description:
Integer overflow in signed arithmetic

Signed integer overflow is undefined behavior in C. The result can wrap around, produce incorrect values, or cause the compiler to make unexpected optimizations.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Adding large values
int x = INT_MAX;
int y = x + 1; // overflow — undefined behavior

// Cause 2: Multiplication overflow
int a = 100000;
int b = 100000;
int c = a * b; // overflow

// Cause 3: Negative shift
int val = -1;
unsigned int shifted = val << 3; // undefined behavior

How to Fix

Fix 1: Check before operation

#include <limits.h>

int safe_add(int a, int b) {
 if (a > 0 && b > INT_MAX - a) {
 fprintf(stderr, "overflow\n");
 return INT_MAX;
 }
 return a + b;
}

Fix 2: Use unsigned types when possible

unsigned int x = UINT_MAX;
unsigned int y = x + 1; // well-defined wrap

Fix 3: Use compiler built-ins

#include <stdint.h>

int32_t result;
if (__builtin_add_overflow(a, b, &result)) {
 fprintf(stderr, "overflow\n");
}

Related Errors

  • Division by zero — floating point exception.
  • Null pointer dereference — null pointer crash.
  • Out of memory — allocation failure.

item.title: [Solution] C Use after free — heap memory
item.description:
Use after free — heap memory

Use-after-free occurs when a program continues to use a pointer after the memory it points to has been freed. This is undefined behavior that can lead to crashes, data corruption, or security vulnerabilities.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Accessing freed memory
int *p = malloc(sizeof(int));
*p = 42;
free(p);
printf("%d\n", *p); // use-after-free

// Cause 2: Using freed pointer in function
char *get_string(void) {
 char *s = malloc(100);
 strcpy(s, "hello");
 free(s);
 return s; // dangling pointer
}

// Cause 3: Use-after-free in data structure
struct Node {
 int data;
 struct Node *next;
};
// free node but still reference node->next

How to Fix

Fix 1: Set pointer to NULL after free

int *p = malloc(sizeof(int));
*p = 42;
free(p);
p = NULL; // safe

Fix 2: Don’t return freed pointers

char *get_string(void) {
 char *s = malloc(100);
 strcpy(s, "hello");
 return s; // caller must free
}

Fix 3: Use Valgrind to detect

gcc -g -o prog prog.c
valgrind --tool=memcheck ./prog

Examples

#include <stdlib.h>
#include <stdio.h>

int main(void) {
 int *arr = malloc(5 * sizeof(int));
 if (!arr) return 1;
 
 for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
 arr[i] = i * 10;
 }
 
 free(arr);
 arr = NULL; // prevent use-after-free
 
 // Don't access arr here
 
 return 0;
}

Related Errors

  • Double free — freeing twice.
  • Heap corruption — general heap damage.
  • Invalid free — freeing invalid pointer.

item.title: [Solution] C Use of uninitialized variable
item.description:
Use of uninitialized variable

Using an uninitialized variable leads to undefined behavior. The variable contains whatever garbage value happened to be at that memory location.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Local variable not initialized
int x;
printf("%d\n", x); // undefined behavior

// Cause 2: Uninitialized pointer
int *ptr;
*ptr = 10; // crash

// Cause 3: Partial initialization
int arr[10];
arr[0] = 1;
printf("%d\n", arr[5]); // undefined behavior

How to Fix

Fix 1: Initialize at declaration

int x = 0;
printf("%d\n", x); // safe

Fix 2: Use calloc for arrays

int *arr = calloc(10, sizeof(int)); // zero-initialized
printf("%d\n", arr[5]); // safe — 0
free(arr);

Fix 3: Initialize all struct members

struct Point {
 int x, y;
};
struct Point p = { 0, 0 }; // initialize all members

Examples

#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>

int main(void) {
 int count = 0; // initialize
 double values[100] = {0}; // zero-initialize array
 
 for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
 values[i] = (double)i;
 count++;
 }
 
 printf("Count: %d\n", count);
 return 0;
}

Related Errors

  • Segmentation fault — crash from uninitialized pointer.
  • Integer overflow — arithmetic overflow.
  • Format string vulnerability — format string issue.

item.title: [Solution] C++ UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer - UB detected
item.description:
UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer - UB detected

UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer (UBSan) detects undefined behavior such as signed integer overflow, null pointer dereference, and misaligned pointer access.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Signed integer overflow
int x = INT_MAX;
int y = x + 1; // UBSan: signed integer overflow

// Cause 2: Null pointer dereference
int* p = nullptr;
*p = 10; // UBSan: null pointer use

// Cause 3: Misaligned access
char buf[16];
int* p = reinterpret_cast<int*>(buf + 1);
*p = 42; // UBSan: misaligned address

How to Fix

Fix 1: Compile with UBSan

g++ -fsanitize=undefined -g -o prog prog.cpp
./prog

Fix 2: Fix undefined behavior

// Fix signed overflow
int x = INT_MAX;
if (x > INT_MAX - 1) {
 // handle overflow
}
int y = x + 1;

// Fix null pointer
if (p != nullptr) {
 *p = 10;
}

Fix 3: Use unsigned for wrapping

unsigned int x = UINT_MAX;
unsigned int y = x + 1; // well-defined wrap

Related Errors

  • AddressSanitizer — memory errors.
  • MemorySanitizer — uninitialized memory.
  • std::overflow_error — arithmetic overflow.

item.title: [Solution] UBSan Undefined Behavior Detected Fix
item.description:
UBSan Undefined Behavior Detected

Fix UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer detected errors. Handle signed integer overflow, null pointer dereference, and alignment issues.

What This Error Means

UBSan detects undefined behavior at runtime:

runtime error: signed integer overflow: 2147483647 + 1 cannot be represented in type 'int'
runtime error: null pointer passed as argument 2, which is declared to never be null
runtime error: load of misaligned address 0x... for type 'int', which requires 4 byte alignment

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Signed integer overflow
int x = INT_MAX + 1; // UB!

// Cause 2: Null pointer dereference
int* p = nullptr;
*p = 42; // UB!

// Cause 3: Shift overflow
int y = 1 << 32; // UB - shift amount >= width of type

// Cause 4: Misaligned access
char buf[8];
int* ip = reinterpret_cast<int*>(buf + 1);
*ip = 42; // UB - misaligned

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use unsigned types for bit operations

#include <cstdint>

uint32_t shift_value(uint32_t val, int shift) {
 if (shift >= 32) return 0;
 return val << shift;
}

Fix 2: Check for null before dereferencing

void process(int* ptr) {
 if (ptr == nullptr) {
 return;
 }
 *ptr = 42;
}

Fix 3: Use safe arithmetic

#include <limits>
#include <stdexcept>

int safe_add(int a, int b) {
 if (b > 0 && a > std::numeric_limits<int>::max() - b) {
 throw std::overflow_error("Integer overflow");
 }
 if (b < 0 && a < std::numeric_limits<int>::min() - b) {
 throw std::underflow_error("Integer underflow");
 }
 return a + b;
}

Examples

#include <cstdint>
#include <limits>
#include <stdexcept>

class SafeInt {
 int value_;

public:
 explicit SafeInt(int v) : value_(v) {}

 SafeInt operator+(const SafeInt& other) const {
 int64_t result = static_cast<int64_t>(value_) + other.value_;
 if (result > std::numeric_limits<int>::max() ||
 result < std::numeric_limits<int>::min()) {
 throw std::overflow_error("Integer overflow in addition");
 }
 return SafeInt(static_cast<int>(result));
 }

 int value() const { return value_; }
};

int main() {
 try {
 SafeInt a(std::numeric_limits<int>::max());
 SafeInt b(1);
 SafeInt c = a + b; // Throws
 } catch (const std::overflow_error& e) {
 // Handled safely
 }
 return 0;
}

Related Errors

  • Sanitizers Undefined — UBSan error
  • Sanitizers Address — ASan error
  • Overflow Error — overflow error
